description: Distribution market on the US general tariff rate on Chinese imports as of July 1, 2026. The below-10% bucket at 7% represents the de-escalation scenario. Cross-platform comparison with Polymarket's March 31 distribution shows the term structure of tariff expectations.
question: Will the tariff rate on China imports be below 10% on July 1, 2026?

resolution_logic.summary: Resolves Yes if the general import tariff rate on Chinese imports is below 10% on July 1, 2026. Rate includes both universal US tariffs and China-specific tariffs.

triggers.description: General import tariff rate on China below 10%
triggers.metric: General import tariff rate on China

resolution_logic.ambiguity_notes: Kalshi uses 'in effect (i.e., being collected)' language — stricter than Polymarket's 'in effect' definition. Multiple general rates sum: e.g., 10% reciprocal + 10% fentanyl = 20%. The examples in the rules (India 50%, China 20%, EU 15%) provide concrete precedent for how stacking works. Below 10% requires significant de-escalation from current ~10-20% levels.

catalyst_dates.event: Polymarket March 31 tariff rate snapshot — near-term price signal
G7 summit — potential trade negotiation catalyst

resolution_source.name: Trump administration / US Customs and Border Protection

cross_platform_link.structural_notes: Polymarket has March 31 tariff distribution (5-15% bucket at 92%). Different date (March vs July) and different bucket boundaries (5-15% vs below 10%). Cross-platform comparison shows term structure: market expects current ~10% rate to hold through March (92% in 5-15% bucket) but assigns 7% to de-escalation below 10% by July — three more months of potential trade deals.

related_markets.notes: China tariff 30-40% on July 1 at 5% — escalation scenario. Full distribution: below 10% (7%), 10-20% implied dominant, 30-40% (5%), 40-50% (3%), 50-60% (4%), above 60% (8%)
